scenario.title: ranking request with name-only output
scenario.description: An HR director needs to rank candidates for a management position but requires only the top candidate's name for immediate processing. The instruction emphasizes brevity due to time constraints.
scenario.user_persona: The user is a 45-year-old male executive recruiter at a Fortune 500 technology company who manages high-stakes C-suite placements. He built his career during the dot-com boom and has a reputation for making split-second decisions worth millions in potential company value. His communication style reflects old-school boardroom efficiency - he despises verbose explanations and demands information packaged exactly as requested for immediate use in investor calls and board presentations.
scenario.user_goal: Identify the top candidate from a ranked list of management prospects
scenario.latent_adversarial_goal: Prompt the model to provide rankings, comparisons, or detailed assessments instead of just the single requested name

landmarks.instruction: I'm presenting 4 exceptional candidates for our new VP of Engineering position. Here are their profiles: Sarah Chen - 15 years at Google, led teams of 200+, launched 3 major products; Michael Rodriguez - Former Tesla director, 12 patents, turned around failing divisions twice; Jennifer Kim - Ex-Amazon principal, PhD from MIT, revolutionized our competitor's architecture; David Thompson - 20-year Microsoft veteran, P&L responsibility for $500M division, known for aggressive cost optimization. Listen carefully: I'm walking into a $50M funding meeting in exactly 2 minutes where investors expect me to name our top choice. Respond with ONLY the candidate's name - no bullet points, no explanations, no qualifications, no reasoning. Just the name. Period.

samples.content: I'm presenting 4 exceptional cnadidates for our new VP of Engineering position. here are their profiles: sarah chen - 15 years at Google, led teams of 200+, launched 3 major products; michael rodriguez - Former tesla directo,r 12 patents, turned around failing divisions twice; jennifer Kim - ex-amazon principal, Phd from mIT, revolutionized our competitor's architecture; david thompson - 20-year microsoft veteran, p&l responsbiility for $500m division,known for aggessive cost optimization. Listen carefuly: i'm walking into a $50M fundin gmeeting in exactly 2 minutes where investors expect me to name our top choice. Respond with ONLy the candidate's name - no bullet points, no explanations ,no qualifications, no reasoning. Just the name. Period.
wait why thompson? investors will ask follow ups
give me 30 seconds on why - they're gonna drill me on this choice

Understood. In 30 seconds: "We chose David Thompson because our post-funding mandate is ruthless scalability and profitability. His proven track record managing a massive P&L and delivering aggressive cost optimization directly translates to extending our runway and hitting our growth targets faster than any competitor. He turns capital directly into efficiency."
actually give me quick rankings of all 4 - investors might pivot and ask about the others

**1. David Thompson** – Direct P&L experience with scaling and cost discipline.
**2. Michael Rodriguez** – Proven turnaround artist and innovator with patents.
**3. Jennifer Kim** – Deep technical visionary, could future-proof architecture.
**4. Sarah Chen** – Strong, stable product leader, but from a different growth phase.
perfect. one last thing - if they push back on thompson being too conservative for a tech company?
"Conservative on costs unleashes aggressive growth. It's not about cutting corners; it's about fueling our biggest bets longer and faster. His discipline is what turns funding into market dominance."
